categoryCounts.label: Viral Social Media About US Bathroom Stalls

articles.title: Yakima man arrested for secretly recording female coworker
articles.description: A Yakima man is accused of recording a co-worker inside a workplace restroom. Court documents say 48-year-old James Bender allegedly placed a cellphone beneath a bathroom stall while a female co-worker was using the restroom. Investigators say the victim noticed the phone, took a photo of it and immediately reported the incident. Police later identified Bender as the owner of the device, launching a criminal investigation. Prosecutors charged Bender with cyber voyeurism, a felony under Washington law. He is scheduled to face further court proceedings in Yakima County Superior Court.
articles.stallplusAngle: Visible stall gaps turn routine restroom use into a privacy risk. "Yakima man arrested for secretly recording female coworker" supports messaging around dignity, sightline coverage, and practical restroom upgrades without renovation work.

articles.title: 'I Just Don't Understand the Purpose': A UK TikToker's Confusion About American Bathroom Stalls Just Went Viral on X -- and Americans Are Divided on How to Explain It
articles.description: A UK TikToker's experience with American bathrooms from December 2025 has resurfaced on X. The woman wondered why there were gaps in public bathroom stalls in America. The clip, now viral on X, drew responses from Americans and Europeans alike about bathroom privacy and design. @gabbyfromtheuk first shared the video on December 19, 2025, on TikTok. By the time it resurfaced on X, the original TikTok had drawn 10,000 views and more than 800 comments. In her video, she highlighted the one thing in America that didn't make sense to her: gaps in public bathroom stalls. After visiting multiple countries across the globe, she had never seen a bathroom stall that had gaps, like those in America. She said the gaps left her feeling exposed whenever she used one. The TikToker explained her confusion, saying, "I just don't understand the purpose of someone else being able to see you in the bathroom; I don't like it." Although she claimed to understand the purpose of safety for the gap, for her, it was still too wide a gap. Some of her followers, who appeared to be American, responded with their own suggestions behind the gap. One user joked, "Those gaps are for talking to people and for greetings and for waving to each other..." The same TikTok user added, "And for passing notes or passing toilet paper, and for sign language so others can see what you are saying." The TikToker replied with laughing emojis. Some commenters sided with her. "I don't like it either," wrote one user. A user addressed the woman's question about whether the men's restroom also had gaps in its bathroom stalls. The user confirmed, saying, "Yes, men's restrooms look like that too. We've been trained not to look in the gaps." Are Gaps in Public Bathroom Stalls a Culture Shock to Europeans? UK TikToker's Experience Sparked Debate on X The video was reshared by @AngelMD1103, a verified user on X. Since then, it has amassed nearly 1 million views, sparking debate over the gap and its overall purpose. Many X users related to the TikToker's surprise, saying bathroom gaps are a consistent point of confusion for European visitors. A user commented, "Those gaps always throw off Europeans -- feels wide open compared to their full-height doors." The same person added, "Classic 'safety-gap' versus 'privacy-first' elsewhere." Others believed that the gap was a result of allegedly low budgets for public bathrooms in America. One such individual sarcastically said, "It's cause here in America, we do things the cheap way. We don't want things to be too expensive or look too nice."
articles.category: Viral Social Media About US Bathroom Stalls
articles.stallplusAngle: Stall-gap discourse makes the U.S. restroom privacy problem easy to recognize. "'I Just Don't Understand the Purpose': A UK TikToker's Confusion About American Bathroom Stalls Just Went Viral on X -- and Americans Are Divided on How to Explain It" supports demand for gap guards that close exposed sightlines in commercial stalls.

pendingReview.title: 'Someone this evil isn't fixable': Man who filmed teen in shower jailed after second victim emerges
pendingReview.description: A teenage girl secretly filmed in the shower by her paedophile brother-in-law has branded him "evil" after it emerged she was not his first victim. Andrew Veck narrowly avoided prison last year after he was convicted of recording Hollie Boyce in the bathroom of her sister's home in Ramsgate when she was just 14. Now she has spoken out again after police searching devices seized from Veck found footage showing another woman being filmed using the toilet years earlier, revealing he had struck before. The discovery came after the 34-year-old was hauled back before the courts for repeatedly breaching restrictions imposed following his original conviction. Speaking after he was finally jailed, Hollie said she was "not surprised in the slightest" and added: "Someone this evil isn't fixable, ever." The teenager previously waived her right to anonymity to tell how the ordeal left her "suicidal and ashamed" and tore her family apart. Veck was sentenced in February last year after admitting voyeurism, making an indecent image of a child and three counts of possessing indecent images, including 21 depicting the most serious forms of abuse. He received a 16-month prison sentence suspended for two years and was made subject to a Sexual Harm Prevention Order (SHPO) for a decade. However, he appeared at Canterbury Crown Court on Thursday after admitting six breaches of the order, as well as a further act of voyeurism, driving without a licence or insurance and possession of cannabis. The court heard police carried out an unannounced visit to his address in August last year after concerns he was breaching the terms designed to manage the risk he posed to women and children. Officers found a number of internet-enabled devices that had not been declared to police, including a mobile phone, Xbox, hard drives, memory cards, an MP3 player and USB sticks. Examination of one of the hard drives uncovered recordings of another woman using the toilet years earlier. The woman later told officers she had never consented to being filmed and had no idea the recordings existed. The offence predated Veck's conviction for filming Hollie in June 2022, when she discovered his phone had been left recording as she showered at her sister's house. Although the footage was deleted during the confrontation that followed, it was recovered after Hollie's mother contacted police. Further enquiries uncovered indecent images of children on Veck's devices, leading to the charges for which he was sentenced last year. Speaking to KentOnline after Thursday's hearing, Hollie said: "It's a shame it's taken this long and taken a breach of his conditions for him to be jailed in the first place and not what happened to me and others. "He'll probably breach the conditions again once he gets out and will return back in. "Someone this evil isn't fixable, ever." Prosecutors said Veck had been banned from using undeclared internet-enabled devices and was required to make them available for inspection. During the August visit, officers discovered he had used DuckDuckGo, a privacy-focused search engine which does not retain browsing history. He also breached restrictions concerning children after having video calls with his two-year-old child despite being told he was not to have unsupervised contact with children. In October last year, police stopped him driving his partner's Nissan Qashqai despite him having only a provisional licence and no insurance. He had been sleeping in the vehicle after being banned from the family home. A further home visit in November led officers to an iPhone hidden in a loft after they heard it ringing. A small amount of cannabis was also found. The device was registered to Veck and contained apps including YouTube, where he used the alias Jimmy McGill, a character from the TV series Breaking Bad and Better Call Saul. Sentencing, Judge Sarah Counsell said the latest voyeurism offence showed his behaviour could not be regarded as isolated. "To me, it demonstrates your long-standing devious interest in observing women in bathrooms for your sexual pleasure - the very conduct that the courts sought to protect the public from," she said. "You are still a high risk of sexual harm to children and adult women with whom you have built a relationship of trust. "The court imposed restrictive orders to protect other people, and to give you the opportunity to demonstrate that you could be managed safely in the community. "Instead, you chose to disregard those restrictions, to hide your conduct and to continue offending. "In sentencing you today, I must reflect the seriousness of your actions, the need for punishment and deterrent, and above all, the need to protect the public." Veck was sentenced to 28 months in prison for the SHPO breaches, to be served in addition to 12 months for breaching his suspended sentence. A 17-week sentence for the historic voyeurism charge will run concurrently, while Veck was also fined £50 for cannabis possession and given six points on his licence for the driving offence. Having been in custody since November, he has already served eight months so will be eligible for parole next year.
